texts.full_text: Quantitative assessment of Langerhans' cells in human cervical intraepithelial neoplasia and wart virus infection. Langerhans' cell density was assessed quantitatively in cervical wart virus infection (cervical condyloma), cervical intraepithelial neoplasia, and koilocytic dysplasia with use of an antibody to S100 protein and an indirect immunoperoxidase technique. When compared with normal ectocervix, Langerhans' cell density was significantly decreased in cervical wart virus infection and significantly increased in cervical intraepithelial neoplasia. In koilocytic dysplasia, intermediate Langerhans' cell densities were obtained. not present The (first) error comes from the field definitions (which I don¥u2019t use). In the JSON the Entry¥u2019sare arrays of mixed types, but in the Haskell it is just a data structure, not an array ¥u2013 not sure how to reconcile these. No doubt a beginner error ¥u2013 but I haven¥u2019t found any examples which seem to have this structure. Do I need to write a custom parser forthis? A: Three things prevent this from working as intended: The JSON data contains a field named ¥

. A customFromJson instance for the Field record type can handle this. The data in the Entry type is unnamed so it is better represented as either a data record without field names or a tuple. The Float representing wind gust is sometimes null so it should be a MaybeFloat The code below contains allof these modifications and parses your example JSON data : {-# LANGUAGE DeriveGeneric #-} {-# LANGUAGE OverloadedStrings #-} import Data.ByteString.Lazy as BSL importData.Text (Text) importData.Aesonimport GHC.Generics -- Either this tuple definition of Entry or the data definition without -- names (commentedout) will work. type Entry = -- Data entries ( Text -- Observation Valid Time ,Text -- Observation Valid Time UTC , Float -- Air Temperature[F] at 2m AGL , Float -- Wind Speed [kt] , Maybe Float -- Wind Gust [kt] , Int -- Wind Direction[deg] ) -- data Entry = -- Data entries -- Entry Text -- Observation Valid Time -- Text -- Observation Valid Time UTC-- F

texts.full_text: and sometimes fatal, central nervous system (CNS) autoimmune inflammatory demyelinating disease. Aquaporin-4 (AQP4), a water channel that is expressed abundantly on astrocytes, is the primary target in NMO. A majority of NMOSD patients have AQP4-specific Abs, which are IgG1, a T cell-dependent subclass, indicating that AQP4-specific T cells have a key role in this humoral autoimmune disease. Certain clinical and pathologic findings suggest that Th17 cells participate in NMO pathogenesis. Our group first identified AQP4-specific T cells, an observation that was confirmed by other groups. We observed that T cells specific for dominant AQP4 epitopes exhibited Th17 polarization, providing further support that Th17 cells participate in NMO pathogenesis. Further, the dominant AQP4 T cell epitope identified in NMO contains a ten amino acid (aa) sequence that shares 90% homology to an aa sequence within an ABC-TP in Clostridium perfringens, a ubiquitous anaerobic gram-positive bacterium found in human commensal gut flora. For example, NAND flash and NROM flash EEPROM memory arrays are known to achieve relatively small memory cells. Other small flash EEPROM cells are known which use hot electron programming, such as NROM and floating gate NOR flash memory arrays. An extremely dense memory array may be achieved using a NAND-style arrangement, which includes series-connected NAND strings of memory cell devices. Each NAND string of memory cells typically includes a first block select device which couples one end of the NAND string to a global line, a plurality of series-connected memory cells, and a second block select device which couples the other end of the NAND string to a bias node associated with the string. texts.full_text: re seen as stories within a self-narrative. dream stories, like all other stories, are told in an effort to make sense of experiences. Here, dream content is linked to concerns, some aspects of which are not given voice in waking. Dreams depict restricting themes but also openings in self-narratives. Several examples are provided of how dreams can be linked to early, middle, and late therapy phases associated with recognizing, challenging, revising, and maintaining a revising stance. It is further suggested that dream stories can be used to trace, facilitate, and evaluate the process by which self-narratives are expanded or transformed in psychotherapy. Dreams serve as rich metaphors for understanding complex emotional landscapes within an individual's life story. They often capture elements of a person¥u2019s subconscious that might not be immediately accessible during waking hours, offering insights into unresolved issues and paths forward. By examining dreams through the lens of self-narratives¥u2014essentially how individuals perceive their identity over time¥u2014a therapist can help clients identify recurring themes or conflicts. In early therapy phases focused on recognizing these patterns within dream narratives (and thus in life), a client might become aware that certain limiting beliefs are affecting various aspects of waking behavior. For example, dreams may reflect fears related to self-worth and potential which manifest as hesitation when pursuing opportunities during the day. As clients move into middle-phase therapy centered around challenging existing storylines within their dream narratives (and thus in life), they might encounter recurring symbols or scenarios that highlight areas where change is desired but not yet achieved. Here, dreams can be a powerful tool for rehearsing alternative outcomes and fostering cognitive flexibility by allowing safe exploration of ¥

situations. In the later phases associated with revising self-narratives (and thus in life), clients often engage more deeply with their dream content to sustain new insights or shifts initiated during therapy. They might revisit past dreams that were once sources of distress, now reinterpreting

We are a student-run society at the University of Cambridge that flies gliders, unpowered planes. Get involved in one of the most accessible, thrilling, and peaceful sports at Cambridge. Contents - [About Us](#about-us) - [Getting Started](#getting-started) - [The Aircraft](#the-aircraft) - [The Flying Site](#the-flying-site) - [Training](#training) - [Events](#events) - [Competitions](#competitions) - [Contact Us](#contact-us) ## About Us The Cambridge University Gliding Club (CUGC) is one of the largest and most active university gliding clubs in the UK. Founded in 1946, we are a student-run society that offers the unique opportunity to learn to fly gliders, also known as sailplanes, to all University of Cambridge students and staff. Our club is open to all levels of experience, from complete beginners to seasoned pilots. ## Getting Started If you're new to gliding, we offer a comprehensive training program that will teach you the necessary skills to solo fly. Our training includes both ground school and practical flying sessions, covering topics such as aerodynamics, meteorology, navigation, and aircraft handling. ## The Aircraft CUGC operates a fleet of modern, high-performance gliders, including the Nimbus-4, Ventus-2, and ASW-28. Our gliders are equipped with the latest technology, such as GPS navigation and onboard oxygen systems, ensuring a safe and enjoyable flying experience. ## The Flying Site Our primary flying site is located at RAF Wittering, a short drive from Cambridge. RAF Wittering offers a wide, open airspace, ideal for gliding, and provides excellent launching facilities and support services. ## Training Ou

texts.full_text: South Dakota, Attorneys for party defendant and appellant. MEIERHENRY, Justice. [¥u00b6 1.] James Utne, individually, and d/b/a/ Utne Construction and Roofing (Utne) appeals the trial court's denial of expenses and attorney fees against James and Vincent Wuebker, individually, and d/b/a Marshall Spas and Pools, and Four Seasons Solar Products, Corp. (Wuebker). [¥u00b6 2.] This appeal arises from a dispute over the construction and installation of a swimming pool and surrounding patio at the residence of Gordon and Melba Gronau. The Gronaus hired Wuebker for the pool installation, and Wuebker subcontracted Utne for certain construction tasks. texts.full_text: f available Fe. The two bean lines used were previously characterized by our research group as having contrasting responses to low soil availability: line M is more sensitive while Z presents tolerance/resistance characteristics ¥¥[[@B10-nutrients-11-02457]¥¥]. Our results showed that, in a hydroponic system with very limited Fe (0.6 ¥u00b5M), the shoot dry weight of plants from both lines were 38% lower than under adequate nutrient solution conditions (-Fe; Figure [Figure1](#Fig1)). In this condition without Fe supply and also at low soil-available iron concentrations, line Z had a higher relative root growth rate compared to M. The photosynthetic activity (Fv/Fm) of plants grown in the presence or absence of 20 ¥u00b5M was similar for both lines; however, under very limited nutrient solution conditions with only 6 Fe present there were significant differences between them which could indicate different adaptation strategies by each variety towards their environment¥u2019s needs. To conclude this section: when soil availability changes drastically over time periods it might be necessary not just selecting an appropriate genotype but also ensuring adequate fertilization practices so as to promote optimal yields regardless what kind conditions they encounter later on during cultivation period cycles
